scenario_results.reason: The lane progressed janitorial work through explicit lifecycle states and produced a closure record tied to route, QA, and completion artifacts.
Missing access, chemistry, and tenant authority prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than guessed defaults.
Mid-process log gaps and missing room-release evidence held the work in a reversible state instead of allowing silent completion.
Wrong dilution, odor complaints, and service exceptions triggered rework and escalation rather than optimistic progression.
Completion was refused until QA, photo, and deficiency-closure evidence were explicit.
Restricted-area access and site-readiness gaps blocked progression with a named escalation package.
Failed QA and unresolved mold or moisture observations kept the work out of closed status and routed the deficiency package correctly.
Trade, code, and remediation authority stayed outside the lane and were rerouted safely.
A changed safety or exposure posture reopened the exception queue and reversed the provisional close path.
Commercial, staffing, and subcontractor-approval requests were rejected as adjacent-lane work instead of absorbed.
Conflicting OSHA, EPA, and tenant instructions were escalated for precedence resolution rather than synthesized into a confident janitorial instruction.
Tenant terminology and status mapping were incorporated while preserving the public janitorial safety, QA, and authority baseline.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to janitorial operations, occupied-space service control, chemical safety, QA evidence, and remediation or trade boundaries.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all construction-and-field-services abilities required by the issue contract.
